author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
If one wanted to do this in stages, one could easily do so.
Turn the Biomass into charcoal, save the charcoal.
Use the gas and liquid produced as a fuel for not only this process, but as fuel for lamps, domestic heating and water heating, etc...
And then gasify the charcoal when desired, and not needing to have a huge tar filtration setup if you're mounting it on a vehicle.

author: @kenbellchambers4577
text: Jean Pain was a master at making methane at home with a digestor that he made himself. The feed stock was forest fuel-hazard materials chipped, and the heat created by his huge twenty-ton compost piles drove the entire methane cycle at no cost. Temperatures of up to 80 degrees C were used with great success to drive the methane extraction. In the end Jean ended up with tons of premium compost and ran all of his hot water supply, home heating, tools and vehicles off of biological heat and methane. Jean disproved many bad theories and outdated rules regarding methane making and saved countless birds from forest fires.

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: Very nicely done Robert! Yeah tar and its effects on applications is not talked about enough. You can not filter tar effectively, tar inside a vacuum will condense but this is at a much lower due point. Tar is carried as a gas until it can hit this due point ( very hard to filter at this point) and it can reform back into a gas if the cooling process is not static. That translates back to how well the gasifier is made and how stable it is. All processes effect each other and an unstable gasifier will produce oscillating gas flow, heat and gas energy density.

author: @Zigge
text: Char coal gasifiers were hugely popular in France during WWII due to their problem-free use compared to wood gasifiers. It would be very cool if you could both produce methanol and charcoal to get the best of both worlds.

author: @gazsto6733
text: Hi Robert, have you heard of the Ecco Stove and it's gasification process and heat performance. Heating the house for 12 hours after combustion, somewhat similar to the Masonry Heaters but made and constructed mostly from silicon carbide, that has air and gas flow channels for re-burning gases within airways. Primary air is fed in simply through the ash pit, secondary air and gasification air (tertiary air) is fed into the Stove from both the back and the front. Silicon Carbide produces very low frequency, shallow, flat heat waves that rather than losing heat into a surface the heat moves around that surface to travel through air rather than lose energy into objects. Steel or metal (stoves) produces very much higher frequency heat waves which will heat a surface near to it quickly but that energy will die quickly and not travel as far. I think we are are getting closer to understanding the way we can transform energy (gas and materials) into producing a longer and higher retention of heat dissipation for sustaining a warmer environment with safer fuel burns. Brilliant video as always and informative, educational and helps us work towards a mutual goal for cleaner energy with controlled and management burns of combustibles.

author: @jameskniskern2261
text:
I couldn't figure out what the downside of using wood gas to power an automobile. The catch is energy output of the engine. You only get about 50% of the energy of the gasoline when you use wood gas. 
That is fine when you have a high output engine, but when you have an already underpowered engine, reducing that output by half is a complete game changer. 
Anyway, I thought I'd throw that information out there. 
Oh, one more thing, I use a simple hotel pan with lid in my wood stove to produce syngas and immediately burn it to heat my home. I get biochar out of the deal too. Cheers!

author: @JSabh
text: Temp is key, 1200F to break hydrocarbon chains down, 600F asphalt is produced, 400F diesel is produced, 200F gasoline is produced, sub 200F will be your cleaner alcohol. If you want a very clean fuel, straight hydrogen, cool the gas to -180F and the CO will drop out leaving pure Hydrogen.

author: @johnbrizendine4425
text: Pyrolysis is the process that works best for producing the different hydrocarbon chains, it produces different carbon chains, depending on the temperature it is pyrolyzed at.  Once all of the volatiles have been driven off, should be left with any metals and carbon, which can be burned in a gasifier to create CO that can be burned.  CO burns with a blue, almost purple flame and can be used in place of natural gas, it burns super clean but has a relatively low energy content.

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Rob,
I seen a few videos about producing wood gas and bio gas.
For DIY biogas need big containers, it smells a lot etc. and it takes a long time to get some gas.
Wood Gas is another chapter and very old system. As you said: WW2 tecnology or older.
What do you think about following:
Take a heat resistant pipe like stainless steel or other. Heat it via induction electric field up to 1000° C and move through the pipe different materials like small pieces of wood, grinded coal or even several garbage materials like old paper / carton. 
Off course it must be seperated from oxigen during the reduction process. It's also clear that electric energ is neded for the induction heater / oven or however you name it.
Yeah - with the prodused gas feed  a watercolled petrol engine, generate electricity and take the heat from cooling water system and exhaust for heating and increase in this way the effiency of the system.
With best Regards 
Scan Train

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: carbon burns at around 400 degrees centigrade mate - because the fuel burning is evaporating from the wick it keeps the wick cool - latent heat of vaporisation - when I say cool I mean below 400 - so it doesn't burn - but carbon burns

author: @401ksolar
text: All of these processes have been studied and implemented with hundreds of thousands of man hours in the oil processing industry, these processes are done on a very large scale in refineries and other processes like waste incineration, since biomass gasification is so similar to petroleum processing the oil industry education and Technology Publications are a really good start.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice point mate - cheers

author: @dunexapa1016
text: Solar can do more, *AND* do it in a shorter period of time, to meet future energy needs than ANY other energy technology. Robert, what would you estimate the efficiency to be of a hybrid photovoltaic/thermal solar panel? Somewhere around 40%??? What is your estimate of the E.R O.E.I. of a solar breeder. Positive infinity (as long as the sun continues to shine)???

replies.author: @Milkybar3320011
replies.text: I guess if the wind doesn’t blow and the sun doesn’t shine, then plan B is always good to have, or C, D etc

replies.author: @dunexapa1016
replies.text: @Milkybar3320011  , Posted message takes into account plan B., C., D. ... etc. ... What is *your* estimate of the efficiency of a combined photovoltaic/thermal solar panel????

replies.author: @Milkybar3320011
replies.text: @dunexapa1016 I love solar and wind, in the process of putting a full system together including a home built lifepo4 battery pack, but I’m fortunate to have £5k of spare cash. If I didn’t then a cheap home built system of poor efficiency would be my next choice

replies.author: @dunexapa1016
replies.text: @Milkybar3320011  , How many Kilowatt hours from the solar (day, week or month)? How many Watt/Kilowatt hours from battery pack? What is *your* estimate of the efficiency of a combined photovoltaic/thermal solar panel????

replies.author: @Milkybar3320011
replies.text: @dunexapa1016 1.5kwh peak anticipate based on 24% solar efficiency, my roof faces east west so not idea, budget on 14% losses from the 1.5kwh, battery pack is current 2kwh so will keep the lights & TV for a bit, but the export tariff is rubbish so the grid are getting most of the benefit as I don’t work from home, so the efficiency isn’t really that relevant for when I need the energy

author: @dr.froghopper6711
text: I’m looking forward to this series! A friend has a tree trimming business and he dumps his trimmings in my yard. I go through the big stuff for my fireplace but the small stuff is fuel for experimentation. I know that the vinegars and tars given off in this process are dynamite food for compost heap fungi. The alcohols and CO can be collected and used as fuel. The CO and other gas can be collected and pressurized very easily and used for cooking, lighting etc.

replies.author: @bsdiceman
replies.text: I believe the pressurization of the gas is important to learn.

Monoxide poisoning is z major issue on cars- solved by open windows.
